comments.text: This has been partially resolved by E. Ackelsberg and F. K. Richter in [AcRi26], under the additional assumption that $B$ meets every residue class in $\mathbb{N}$. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Thanks for linking our paper so quickly! Here's a brief summary of our main result with some comments on the assumption about $B$ meeting every residue class in $\mathbb{N}$. The general case does not seem to have any simple characterization using rotations on groups. The problem is that modular obstructions can get in the way. For example, if $A$ is a random subset of the even numbers with density $1/4$, then $A+A$ will be almost all of the even numbers and have density $1/2$. This provides an example of a highly non-structured set satisfying $d(A+A) = 2d(A)$. We bypass such situations by assuming that one of the sets (we take it to be the set $B$) completely avoids modular obstructions. Our main result says that under this assumption that $B$ meets every residue class in $\mathbb{N}$ and $d(A) + d(B) < 1$, one of two things happens: - the sets $A$ and $B$ arise from a rotation on the product of the circle group with a finite cyclic group, or - $A$ is contained in a single residue class mod $h$ for some $h$ and B “looks like” the union of all but one residue classes mod $h$ The precise statement (with a rigorous meaning of what it means for $B$ to “look like” the union of all but one residue classes mod $h$) can be found as Theorem 1.4 in our paper.
